visual_elements.description: Understanding Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors in Linear Algebra
Understanding Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors in Linear Algebra

Eigen values, often denoted as lambda (λ), are scalars associated with a linear transformation represented by a matrix. They reveal the factor by which these transformations stretch or compress space along certain directions.

Eigen values, often denoted as lambda (λ), are scalars associated with a linear transformation represented by a matrix. They reveal the factor by which these transformations stretch or compress space along certain directions.
An eigenvector corresponding to an eigenvalue is a non-zero vector that only scales and doesn't change its direction when acted upon by the matrix representing our system of equations.
An eigenvector corresponding to an eigenvalue is a non-zero vector that only scales and doesn't change its direction when acted upon by the matrix representing our system of equations.

The process begins with finding eigenvalues through solving the characteristic equation det(A - λI) = 0, where A represents our square matrix and I is the identity matrix. Solving this will give us possible eigenvalues.
